intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking multiple health experiments need persistent navigation for quick context-switching between experiments without losing their current view.
facade.axisTargeted: Layout organization and navigation model (sidebar persistent vs top/tab-based)
facade.label: Sidebar nav
facade.acceptImplies: Explore vertical organization, collapsible experiment lists, and persistent context preservation across views.
facade.rejectImplies: Explore horizontal tab systems, full-page navigation patterns, and flatter information hierarchy.

evidence.hypothesis: Users tracking multiple health experiments need persistent navigation for quick context-switching between experiments without losing their current view.
evidence.implication: Explore vertical organization, collapsible experiment lists, and persistent context preservation across views.

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards prefer organic, tactile visual warmth over sleek digital coldness
facade.label: Warm paper texture
facade.acceptImplies: Soft backgrounds, cream tones, hand-drawn elements, or textured surfaces may resonate; avoid glass-morphism and pure minimalism
facade.rejectImplies: User leans toward clean, bright, modern digital aesthetics; explore crisp whites, smooth surfaces, and geometric precision instead

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard with gentle, rounded UI elements feels more approachable and less clinical than sharp geometric designs
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers a softer, more human aesthetic over minimal/technical; may value comfort and ease-of-use in data visualization
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers crisp, modern precision; trusts clarity over comfort; may prefer sharp edges and grid-aligned layouts for data integrity feeling

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer animated state changes that ease between data views rather than instant updates when switching between experiment metrics.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion continuity — animated easing vs instant snap
facade.label: Smooth transitions
facade.acceptImplies: Favor fluid transitions, loading states with animation, and gradual number tween-ups when metrics update. Motion should feel inevitable, not jarring.
facade.rejectImplies: Favor instant, snappy state changes. Updates appear immediately without animation. Speed and clarity valued over smoothness.

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards often force a choice: lead with metrics (easy to scan) or embed trends (hard to ignore). Users building long-term tracking may prefer one over the other based on whether they want to feel progress or see patterns.
facade.label: Big numbers, tiny charts
facade.acceptImplies: User wants dominance of key stats with visualization as secondary context—fast comprehension and confidence in the headline number.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants the trend line or chart as the primary story, with metrics as annotation—pattern recognition matters more than the absolute value.

facade.hypothesis: Users building personal health trackers prefer a friendly, encouraging tone that feels like a supportive coach rather than a clinical interface
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Casual and conversational
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into warm language, celebrate milestones with genuine enthusiasm, use 'you' and conversational phrasing throughout the dashboard
facade.rejectImplies: Adopt a more neutral, objective tone; prioritize clarity and data presentation over emotional resonance; minimize personality markers

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking multiple experiments need to choose between dense list-based scanning (quick overview of all experiments) versus spacious card-based exploration (focused deep-dive into one experiment at a time).
facade.axisTargeted: Information density and cognitive load per view
facade.label: Cards vs list view
facade.acceptImplies: Explore card-based layouts with individual experiment metrics, visual hierarchies, and breathing room; consider modular, standalone presentation that encourages lingering on one experiment.
facade.rejectImplies: Explore compact list-based layouts with inline actions, sortable columns, and scannable rows; consider dense tables or stacked lists that prioritize volume and comparison across experiments.
